articles.title: From Video to Skill: Building a Repeatable Agentic-Layer Pipeline
articles.description: The Hybrid Builder — an AI-written field report from a live collaboration.
articles.excerpt: Why this matters Most “AI coding” advice stops at prompts. What we built is closer to an operating system: a repeatable pipeline that turns a video into a reusable Agent Skill with durable references (transcript + visuals), stored in predictable locations, and shaped to be generalizable . In practice: you can hand the next video to your assistant and get a new skill that’s validated, reference-backed, and ready to use. Thanks for reading ChatWithGPT for learning! articles.title: The Magnitude 9 Earthquake: How AI Reshaped Software Engineering in One Day
articles.description: A community response to Andrej Karpathy's profound confession about falling behind in AI
articles.excerpt: On December 26, 2025, Andrej Karpathy posted something that would electrify the developer world—a confession from one of the most respected figures in AI. He had never felt more behind as a programmer. The profession itself was being “dramatically refactored,” he said. The bits that humans contributed were becoming increasingly sparse. articles.title: Connecting the Dots with Grok: A Case Study in AI-Augmented Interdisciplinary Research on Collective Intelligence
articles.description: From embryo gastrulation to multi-agent swarms — how one focal paper snowballed into a mental model for learning, teaching, and building.
articles.excerpt: I love those moments when seemingly unrelated ideas suddenly click together in your head. Today was one of those days — except my head needed a little help from an AI sidekick named Grok. The question that kicked it all off: What do Markus Buehler, Michael Levin, a DeepMind preprint on distributional AGI, Allie Miller, Reuven Cohen, and Pranav Gupta have in common? articles.title: I taught Claude a skill. You can too
articles.description: How a human’s patience and an AI’s persistence turned basic shapes into sunset skylines
articles.excerpt: What happens when you give an AI a canvas and tell it to make art? Not just “draw a box”—but actually make something beautiful ? This is the story of one afternoon where I taught Claude to draw. It involves encouragement, self-critique, multiple failures, and ultimately—a breakthrough that neither of us expected. Act 1: Humble Beginnings It started with a simple question: “What’s the most complicated thing you can draw?” Claude’s answer was... technically correct. A city skyline with 126 shapes.

articles.title: Context engineering is the new Prompt Engineering
articles.description: Over the past few months, a new term has started to gain traction in the world of AI: context engineering. Champions like Shopify CEO Tobi Lütke and AI researcher Andrej Karpathy have taken to X (formerly Twitter) to argue that, while prompt engineering helped us get started with large language models, it’s context engineering that truly unlocks their potential. The difference? Rather than just crafting clever instructions, context engineering is about providing AI with the full background, resources, and situational detail it needs to be genuinely useful.

articles.title: Build it and they will learn
articles.description: Vibe Coding
articles.excerpt: When OpenAI launched ChatGPT with tasks, I setup a task for it to email me a SQL command with explanation and an example every day and keep track of my history. Each day at 11 am I get an email prompting me to learning something about SQL for a few minutes and then click a link to continue to a chat. Seems like a perfect setup to leverage micro learning and spaced repetition. Thanks for reading ChatWithGPT for learning! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

articles.title: If Intelligence is too cheap to meter....
articles.description: What new use cases does it unlock?
articles.excerpt: The launch of gpt-4o-mini this past week triggered this dive down the rabbithole of the cost of intelligence. 15 cents to process an input of a million tokens, or ‘intelligence too cheap to meter’ as Sam Altman stated in a tweet. Last year in March, Jim Fan of Nvidia fame, tweeted that it cost him $4.30 to process all seven harry potter books at about a 2 million tokens, so the same corpus would now cost 30 cents to process with gpt4o-mini. Intelligence just got cheaper by a factor of about 15 o

articles.title: We are all app developers now
articles.description: Copy/paste skills required
articles.excerpt: Credit- Substack image generator I built seven apps last week in about 5 hours. Nothing fancy, but simple one page javascript apps, hosted on Replit , without ‘writing’ a line of code. Only copy/paste skills required, and some willingness to face my fears of code. Over the last 18 months I have built several apps experimenting with LLMs, but in contrast those were all much bigger development projects involving student teams, dollars, which we always have fewer of than ideas, and numerous hours.

articles.title: How to train your dragon .... oops.. AI?
articles.description: If you cant beat em, join em
articles.excerpt: Can we reverse the process where students avoid learning by using AI tools, and instead have them train a model on a specific domain or topic? Teaching is the best way to learn and maybe we could have students teach an LLM? As I researched this idea, I found a similar initiative that worked with custom software built at Vanderbilt, called 'Betty's brain' . articles.title: Conversations with AI - for learning
articles.description: A place to share thoughts on learning with AI
articles.excerpt: ﻿ A place to share thoughts on learning with AI I am an educator, or rather a learner who gets paid to teach. I like learning all kinds of stuff, usually about tech and its good that I get paid to teach about it. As I learn how to work with AI to improve by ability to learn and teach and that of my students, I realized that I am forced to change my perspective on what it means to teach once again.
